title: Warning Signs For The AI Boom, Anthropic Passes OpenAI, Robinhood’s AI Trading
summary: A discussion podcast covering multiple AI industry concerns including excessive token consumption growth, low shipment rates for token spend, valuation milestones for AI startups, and emerging AI trading applications. Topics span market inefficiencies, circular investment patterns, and privacy-ethics tradeoffs in AI services.
writeup: A Big Technology Podcast Friday edition with Alex and Ranjan Roy of Margins dissects whether runaway AI token spend at large companies signals a structural problem or isolated bad behavior. The episode works through three interlocking threads: enterprises hitting annual token budgets in three months, the mechanics of internal token leaderboard culture (with Meta as the sharpest example), and how a handful of extreme spending events may have inflated Anthropic's reported ARR from $1B in January 2025 to $47B in May 2026. The through-line is that uncapped Claude Code access to engineers without billing visibility produced a predictable disaster, and the media pendulum has swung from hype to dismissal without pausing at accurate.
key_points: Enterprises including Uber and unnamed clients are hitting their annual AI token budgets in as few as three months, with one AI consultant citing a client that spent $500 million in a single month after failing to put usage limits on Claude licenses for employees.

Meta ran internal AI token leaderboards where one employee consumed 60 trillion tokens, which at current API prices would equal $900 million; Ranjan identifies uncapped Claude Code access to engineers with no billing visibility as the primary structural cause of runaway spend across the industry.

Alex and Ranjan distinguish the confirmed examples (Uber COO saying costs are hard to justify) from the murkier ones: Microsoft canceled Copilot Code licenses partly because it has a competing tool, and the Starbucks case was a computer-vision inventory tool, not an LLM deployment.

Anthropic's annualized recurring revenue grew from $1B in January 2025 to $3B in May 2025 and then accelerated to $47B in May 2026; the hosts flag that a single $500M monthly spend, counted at run-rate by Anthropic, could be recorded as $6B in ARR, raising questions about how much of the ARR surge reflects durable productive use versus uncapped internal experimentation.

Both hosts agree the correction was inevitable and is ultimately healthy, but criticize the speed and completeness of the media pendulum swing toward "AI has no value," arguing the reality is a mix of genuine waste and genuine value that requires more careful separation than the current coverage provides.
